[TITLE] Proof-of-randomness protocol for blockchain consensus: a case of Macau algorithms
[ABSTRACT] A proof-of-randomness (PoR) protocol is presented as a fair and low energy-cost consensus mechanism for blockchains. Each network node of a blockchain may use a true random number generator (TRNG) and hash algorism to fulfil the PoR protocol. In this paper, we give the consensus mechanism of the PoR protocol, and classify it into a new kind of randomized algorithms called Macau. The PoR protocol could generate a blockchain without any competition of computing power or stake of cryptocurrency. Besides, we give some advantages of integrating quantum random number generator (QRNG) chips into hardware wallets, and also discuss the way to cooperate with quantum key distribution (QKD) technology.

target_text: This paper proposes a blockchain consensus method called proof of randomness. Instead of making participants compete through heavy computation or coin ownership, each node uses true random numbers and hashing to help decide who can add to the chain. The authors argue that this could make block creation fairer and much less energy intensive than proof-of-work systems. They also frame the method as part of a new class of randomized algorithms, which they call Macau algorithms, and discuss possible links to quantum random-number hardware and quantum key distribution.

[TITLE] Careful Seeding for k-Medois Clustering with Incremental k-Means++ Initialization
[ABSTRACT] K-medoids clustering is a popular variant of k-means clustering and widely used in pattern recognition and machine learning. A main drawback of k-medoids clustering is that an improper initialization can cause it to get trapped in local optima. An improved k-medoids clustering algorithm, called INCKM algorithm, which is the first to apply incremental initialization to k-medoids clustering, was recently proposed to overcome this drawback. The INCKM algorithm requires the construction of a subset of candidate medoids determined by one hyperparameter for initialization, and meanwhile, it always fails when dealing with imbalanced datasets with an incorrect hyperparameter selection. target_text: This paper looks at a common weakness in k-medoids clustering: bad starting points can lead to poor final clusters. The authors propose a new incremental initialization method, INCKPP, that adds cluster centers one at a time using a k-means++ style strategy, so it is less sensitive to hand-tuned settings than earlier methods. They also introduce a faster version, INCKPPsample. Across synthetic and real datasets, including imbalanced ones, the proposed methods produce better clustering results, while the sampled version improves efficiency without giving up much accuracy.

[TITLE] Energy-efficient User Clustering for UAV-enabled Wireless Networks Using EM Algorithm
[ABSTRACT] Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) can be used to provide wireless connectivity to support the existing infrastructure in hot-spots or replace it in cases of destruction. UAV-enabled wireless provides several advantages in network performance due to drone small cells (DSCs) mobility despite the limited onboard energy. However, the problem of resource allocation has added complexity. In this paper, we propose an energy-efficient user clustering mechanism based on Gaussian mixture models (GMM) using a modified Expected-Maximization (EM) algorithm. The algorithm is intended to provide the initial user clustering and drone deployment upon which additional mechanisms can be employed to further enhance the system performance. target_text: This paper studies how to group users and place drones in UAV-based wireless networks more efficiently. The authors propose a clustering method based on Gaussian mixture models and a modified expectation-maximization procedure, meant to serve as an initial deployment step before later optimization. The goal is to reduce power use and interference while keeping connections reliable. In their experiments, the method improved energy efficiency by about 25 percent and link reliability by about 18.3 percent compared with baseline approaches.

target_text: This paper studies where roadside LiDAR sensors should be placed so that vehicle-to-everything perception systems work better. The authors build a realistic LiDAR simulation library inside CARLA and use it to compare different installation layouts with multiple detection models. Their pipeline searches for stronger placements and then analyzes how point-cloud density and uniformity relate to perception quality. In the standard lane setting they report about a 15 percent gain in average precision over a conventional placement scheme, suggesting that better sensor placement alone can noticeably improve detection performance.

[TITLE] Discovering Structural Hole Spanners in Dynamic Networks via Graph Neural Networks
[ABSTRACT] Structural Hole (SH) theory states that the node which acts as a connecting link among otherwise disconnected communities gets positional advantages in the network. These nodes are called Structural Hole Spanners (SHS). SHSs have many applications, including viral marketing, information dissemination, community detection, etc. Numerous solutions are proposed to discover SHSs; however, most of the solutions are only applicable to static networks. Since real-world networks are dynamic networks; consequently, in this study, we aim to discover SHSs in dynamic networks. Discovering SHSs is an NP-hard problem, due to which, instead of discovering exact k SHSs, we adopt a greedy approach to discover top-k SHSs. target_text: This paper focuses on finding structural hole spanners, the nodes that act as bridges between otherwise weakly connected groups in a changing network. Because finding the exact best set is computationally hard, the authors train a graph neural network to identify strong candidates across sequences of network snapshots. The method is designed for dynamic networks rather than fixed ones, which makes it closer to real social and information networks. In experiments, the proposed model was dramatically faster than the comparison method while still aiming to recover the most important bridge nodes.

The authors show that, under suitable conditions, the reconstruction problem can be rewritten as a weighted L1 optimization problem with linear constraints. That matters because it turns the triangulation task into something that can be solved with standard linear programming tools. They also prove that the solution is unique and matches a Delaunay-like triangulation studied in related work.

target_text: This paper is a theoretical result about which hereditary graph classes count as stable. The authors connect ideas from graph decompositions and model theory, and show that stable hereditary graph classes are exactly the ones that admit quasibounded-size decompositions with bounded-shrubdepth base classes. In simpler terms, the paper gives a structural description of an important family of graphs. It also yields consequences about the kinds of large cliques or large independent sets graphs in these classes must contain.

[TITLE] Acceleration of Gossip Algorithms through the Euler-Poisson-Darboux Equation
[ABSTRACT] Gossip algorithms and their accelerated versions have been studied exclusively in discrete time on graphs. In this work, we take a different approach, and consider the scaling limit of gossip algorithms in both large graphs and large number of iterations. target_text: This paper explains accelerated gossip algorithms by connecting them to continuous equations from mathematical physics. In the large-scale limit, the standard gossip method behaves like the heat equation, while an accelerated version behaves like the Euler-Poisson-Darboux equation, a damped wave equation. That difference helps explain why the accelerated method can spread information much faster across a network. The paper also includes simulations showing that these continuous models closely track the behavior of the original discrete algorithms.

[TITLE] Inferring Capabilities from Task Performance with Bayesian Triangulation
[ABSTRACT] As machine learning models become more general, we need to characterise them in richer, more meaningful ways. We describe a method to infer the cognitive profile of a system from diverse experimental data. To do so, we introduce measurement layouts that model how task-instance features interact with system capabilities to affect performance. These features must be triangulated in complex ways to be able to infer capabilities from non-populational data -- a challenge for traditional psychometric and inferential tools. Using the Bayesian probabilistic programming library PyMC, we infer different cognitive profiles for agents in two scenarios: 68 actual contestants in the AnimalAI Olympics and 30 synthetic agents for O-PIAAGETS, an object permanence battery. We showcase the potential for capability-oriented evaluation.

This allows richer inference than task taxonomies or category aggregates, and avoids post-hoc in te rp re ta ti on of statistical factors as “capabilities.” Related Bayesian frameworks such as Hi Bay ES focus on hierarchical GLM modelling of aggregate also identifies capabilities in relation to task demands, but does not allow for the complex combination of demands with non-linear linking functions, limiting its explanatory power compared to measurement layouts. However, these that enables simultaneous inferences about multiple capabilities from patterns of task performance. Appendix E.1 contains a detailed breakdown of inferred parameters of the cognitive profiles of each agent, more information on the experimental set up, and the full qualitative comparing inferred capabilities to the ground truth capabilities of the agents designed by Team A. E.2 contains a deeper of the results, looking at different categories of agent in more detail, and E.3 contains the full forest plots for all agents and core capabilities. In order to link demands and capabilities with task success we need to understand the dependency structure of the task; that is, which demands need to be met in order to succeed.
target_text: This paper proposes a Bayesian way to infer what capabilities a system likely has from how it performs across many tasks. Instead of relying on a single overall score, the method models how task features interact with hidden abilities and then estimates a richer cognitive profile. The authors demonstrate the approach on AnimalAI competitors and on agents tested with object-permanence tasks, showing that the resulting profiles are more informative and more predictive than simple benchmark averages. The main contribution is an evaluation framework for describing systems in terms of capabilities rather than just total performance.

[ABSTRACT] The article aims to analyze the performance of ChatGPT, a large language model developed by OpenAI, in the context of cardiology and vascular pathologies. The study evaluated the accuracy of ChatGPT in answering challenging multiple-choice questions (QCM) using a dataset of 190 questions from the Siamois-QCM platform. The goal was to assess ChatGPT potential as a valuable tool in medical education compared to two well-ranked students of medicine. The results showed that ChatGPT outperformed the students, scoring 175 out of 190 correct answers with a percentage of 92.10\%, while the two students achieved scores of 163 and 159 with percentages of 85.78\% and 82.63\%, respectively. target_text: This study tests ChatGPT on 190 multiple-choice questions in cardiology and vascular pathology from the Siamois-QCM platform. ChatGPT answered 175 correctly, or 92.1 percent, which was higher than the scores of the two strong medical students used for comparison. The paper suggests that large language models could be useful as study aids in medical education, at least for question answering in this domain. At the same time, the authors note that more refinement and further evaluation are still needed before treating such systems as dependable domain-specific tools.

[TITLE] Revitalizing education through ict: a short overview of japan's current landscape
[ABSTRACT] The domain of Information and Communication Technology (ICT) education has garnered significant consideration in recent times. However, several challenges are inherent to this area of study, including monetary expense, temporal factors, pedagogical environment, teacher training programs, incentive, syllabus design, and health-related concerns. This paper presents an analysis of the difficulties encountered in the realm of ICT education in Japan, taking into account ten different perspectives. target_text: This paper gives a short overview of information and communication technology in Japanese education. Rather than proposing a new system, it reviews the main obstacles to broader ICT use, including cost, time, teaching conditions, teacher preparation, incentives, curriculum design, and health concerns. The goal is to highlight the practical barriers that shape how digital tools are adopted in Japan's education system.

[TITLE] Quantum Cross Subspace Alignment Codes via the $N$-sum Box Abstraction
[ABSTRACT] Cross-subspace alignment (CSA) codes are used in various private information retrieval (PIR) schemes (e.g., with secure storage) and in secure distributed batch matrix multiplication (SDBMM). Using a recently developed $N$-sum box abstraction of a quantum multiple-access channel (QMAC), we translate CSA schemes over classical multiple-access channels into efficient quantum CSA schemes over a QMAC, achieving maximal superdense coding gain. Because of the $N$-sum box abstraction, the underlying problem of coding to exploit quantum entanglements for CSA schemes, becomes conceptually equivalent to that of designing a channel matrix for a MIMO MAC subject to given structural constraints imposed by the $N$-sum box abstraction, such that the resulting MIMO MAC is able to implement the functionality of a CSA scheme (encoding/decoding) \emph{over-the-air}.

target_text: This paper translates a family of coding methods used in classical communication into a quantum setting. Using an abstraction called the N-sum box, the authors build quantum cross-subspace alignment codes for quantum multiple-access channels and show how these codes can exploit entanglement efficiently. The work is motivated by applications such as private information retrieval and secure distributed matrix multiplication. In short, the paper provides a way to carry over useful alignment ideas from classical coding into quantum communication protocols.

[TITLE] Audio Contrastive-based Fine-tuning: Decoupling Representation Learning and Classification
[ABSTRACT] Standard fine-tuning of pre-trained audio models couples representation learning with classifier training, which can obscure the true quality of the learned representations. In this work, we advocate for a disentangled two-stage framework that separates representation refinement from downstream evaluation. First, we employ a "contrastive-tuning" stage to explicitly improve the geometric structure of the model's embedding space. Subsequently, we introduce a dual-probe evaluation protocol to assess the quality of these refined representations from a geometric perspective. This protocol uses a linear probe to measure global linear separability and a k-Nearest Neighbours probe to investigate the local structure of class clusters. target_text: This paper argues that fine-tuning an audio model should not mix representation learning and classifier training into one step. The authors propose a two-stage process: first refine the embedding space with contrastive tuning, then evaluate the learned representation with both a linear probe and a k-nearest-neighbors probe. This makes it easier to see whether the representation itself really improved. Across several audio classification tasks, the method outperformed standard fine-tuning and gave a clearer picture of why the learned features were better.

target_text: This paper studies how to test whether an unknown Boolean function is essentially the same as a known one after a linear change of coordinates, even when some errors are allowed. The main result is a much more query-efficient tolerant tester than previous work, along with a stronger lower bound showing that the problem still cannot be solved too cheaply. In practical terms, the paper narrows the gap between what is known to be possible and what is known to be necessary for this kind of invariance testing. It is mainly a theoretical advance in property testing and complexity.

target_text: This paper studies adversarial team games, where several players with the same interests face a single opposing player in a zero-sum setting. The authors resolve the long-open complexity status of computing a standard Nash equilibrium by showing that the problem lies in the CLS class, which in turn gives CLS-completeness together with earlier hardness results. They also prove that an approximate stationary strategy for the team can be turned into an approximate equilibrium in polynomial time. Overall, the paper is a complexity-theoretic result that clarifies how hard these games are to solve.

[TITLE] Networked Communication for Decentralised Agents in Mean-Field Games
[ABSTRACT] Methods like multi-agent reinforcement learning struggle to scale with growing population size. Mean-field games (MFGs) are a game-theoretic approach that can circumvent this by finding a solution for an abstract infinite population, which can then be used as an approximate solution for the $N$-agent problem. However, classical mean-field algorithms usually only work under restrictive conditions. We take steps to address this by introducing networked communication to MFGs, in particular to settings that use a single, non-episodic run of $N$ decentralised agents to simulate the infinite population, as is likely to be most reasonable in real-world deployments. target_text: This paper brings networked communication into mean-field games for decentralized agents. The goal is to keep the scalability benefits of mean-field methods while relaxing the strong assumptions used by more classical approaches. The authors provide theory and empirical results showing that limited communication can substantially improve learning compared with fully independent agents, and can often approach the performance of more centralized methods. They also report gains in robustness when updates fail or when the population size changes.

[TITLE] Agile TLB Prefetching and Prediction Replacement Policy
[ABSTRACT] Virtual-to-physical address translation is a critical performance bottleneck in paging-based virtual memory systems. The Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB) accelerates address translation by caching frequently accessed mappings, but TLB misses lead to costly page walks. Hardware and software techniques address this challenge. Hardware approaches enhance TLB reach through system-level support, while software optimizations include TLB prefetching, replacement policies, superpages, and page size adjustments. Prefetching Page Table Entries (PTEs) for future accesses reduces bottlenecks but may incur overhead from incorrect predictions. Integrating an Agile TLB Prefetcher (ATP) with SBFP optimizes performance by leveraging page table locality and dynamically identifying essential free PTEs during page walks. target_text: This paper focuses on speeding up virtual-memory address translation, where TLB misses can trigger expensive page walks. The authors combine an Agile TLB Prefetcher with a prediction-based replacement policy to better anticipate which page-table entries will be needed and which cached entries are most useful to keep. The overall goal is to reduce translation overhead and improve memory-system performance beyond traditional replacement strategies such as LRU.

[TITLE] Predicting Company Growth using Scaling Theory informed Machine Learning
[ABSTRACT] Predicting company growth is a critical yet challenging task because observed dynamics blend an underlying structural growth trend with volatile fluctuations. Here, we propose a Scaling-Theory-Informed Machine Learning (STIML) framework that integrates a scaling-based growth model to capture the mechanism-driven average trend, together with a data-driven forecasting model to learn the residual fluctuations. Using Compustat annual financial statement data (1950--2019) for 31,553 North American companies, we extend the growth model beyond assets to multiple financial indicators, and evaluate STIML against growth model-only and purely data-driven baselines. Across 16 target variables, we show that company growth exhibits a clear separation between trend-driven predictability and fluctuation-driven predictability, with their relative importance depending strongly on company size and volatility. target_text: This paper tackles the problem of forecasting company growth by combining theory with machine learning. The proposed STIML framework uses a scaling-based model to capture the average structural growth trend and a data-driven model to predict the remaining fluctuations. Using Compustat data from more than 31,000 North American companies across many decades, the authors show that this hybrid approach improves prediction across multiple financial indicators and clarifies how trend and volatility contribute differently depending on company size.

[TITLE] EchoMimicV2: Towards Striking, Simplified, and Semi-Body Human Animation
[ABSTRACT] Recent work on human animation usually involves audio, pose, or movement maps conditions, thereby achieves vivid animation quality. target_text: This paper presents EchoMimicV2, a method for generating expressive half-body human animation from a reference image, an audio clip, and hand-pose input. The system is designed to reduce the number of cumbersome control conditions used by earlier methods while still improving facial motion, gestures, and overall visual quality. The authors report that the approach performs strongly in both quantitative and qualitative evaluations, making simplified yet high-quality human animation more practical.

[TITLE] MSM-BD: Multimodal Social Media Bot Detection Using Heterogeneous Information
[ABSTRACT] Although social bots can be engineered for constructive applications, their potential for misuse in manipulative schemes and malware distribution cannot be overlooked. This dichotomy underscores the critical need to detect social bots on social media platforms. Advances in artificial intelligence have improved the abilities of social bots, allowing them to generate content that is almost indistinguishable from human-created content. These advancements require the development of more advanced detection techniques to accurately identify these automated entities. Given the heterogeneous information landscape on social media, spanning images, texts, and user statistical features, we propose MSM-BD, a Multimodal Social Media Bot Detection approach using heterogeneous information. The authors compare several open-source models, including 8-bit and 4-bit versions, on code-generation benchmarks and static quality checks. They find that smaller and quantized models can still generate usable code, but results are uneven and code quality problems remain, so the outputs should be carefully validated before real software use.

target_text: Timed automata are mathematical models for systems where timing matters, and earlier work showed that checking opacity in these models is generally undecidable. This paper identifies several cases where the problem becomes manageable, including restrictions on the system or the attacker, and it studies the relationship between different notions of opacity. It also proposes new definitions that limit what an attacker can observe, leading to decidability results for the full timed-automata class under those bounded-observation settings.

target_text: This paper revisits a long-running correctness issue in multiparty session types, a framework for checking whether distributed programs follow a communication protocol. Earlier work showed flaws in existing proofs that used end-point projection with mergeability, which raised doubts about soundness. The authors propose a new proof technique based on associating the behavior of a global protocol with its projected local types, and use it to recover soundness and key guarantees such as session fidelity, deadlock freedom, and liveness.

[ABSTRACT] Speech Large Language Models have achieved breakthroughs in multilingual speech-to-text translation. However, existing approaches often overlook semantic commonalities across source languages, leading to biased translation performance. In this work, we propose POTSA (Parallel Optimal Transport for Speech Alignment), a new framework based on cross-lingual parallel speech pairs and Optimal Transport, designed to bridge high- and low-resource translation gaps. First, we introduce a Bias Compensation module to coarsely align initial speech representations. Second, we impose token-level OT constraints on a Q-Former using parallel pairs to establish fine-grained representation consistency. Then, we apply a layer scheduling strategy to focus OT constraints on semantically beneficial layers. target_text: This paper introduces POTSA, a method for improving multilingual speech-to-text translation, especially for languages with less training data. The main idea is to align speech representations across languages so the model can better share useful semantic information instead of treating each source language too separately. The system first applies a coarse bias-compensation step, then uses Optimal Transport on parallel speech pairs to enforce finer alignment, and finally focuses that alignment on the layers where it helps most. On the FLEURS benchmark, the authors report stronger translation quality in both common and zero-shot languages. In practical terms, the work aims to reduce the performance gap between high-resource and low-resource speech translation settings.

target_text: This paper introduces a new kind of programmable resistor for microelectronics. The device can be set to many stable resistance levels across an extremely large range while still keeping a nearly perfectly linear electrical response, which is important for accurate analog computation. The key idea is to use an electrochemical design that spreads heat and material changes through a larger volume instead of concentrating switching in a tiny weak spot. According to the paper, this greatly reduces conductance errors and allows thousands of usable states. The authors also show circuit-style operations such as amplification, division, and multiplication, suggesting the device could be useful for in-memory computing and other compact analog hardware.

[ABSTRACT] Matrix multiplication is a fundamental operation in both training of neural networks and inference. To accelerate matrix multiplication, Graphical Processing Units (GPUs) provide it implemented in hardware. Due to the increased throughput over the software-based matrix multiplication, the multipliers are increasingly used outside of AI, to accelerate various applications in scientific computing. However, matrix multipliers targeted at AI are at present not compliant with IEEE 754 floating-point arithmetic behaviour, with different vendors offering different numerical features. This leads to non-reproducible results across different generations of GPU architectures, at the matrix multiply-accumulate instruction level. To study numerical characteristics of matrix multipliers -- such as rounding behaviour, accumulator width, normalization points, extra carry bits, and others -- test vectors are typically constructed.

target_text: This paper builds highly accurate software models of NVIDIA tensor cores, the specialized hardware units that speed up matrix multiplication on GPUs. Because these units do not always behave like standard IEEE floating-point arithmetic, results can differ across GPU generations and can be hard to reproduce exactly. The authors reverse-engineer important numerical details such as rounding behavior, accumulator width, and normalization rules by designing sensitive test vectors and repeatedly comparing model predictions against real hardware outputs. They report bit-accurate models for several data-center GPUs and common low- or mixed-precision formats. These models can help researchers test numerical methods more reliably and study tensor-core behavior without always needing direct access to every target GPU.

[TITLE] A Modular Reference Architecture for MCP-Servers Enabling Agentic BIM Interaction
[ABSTRACT] Agentic workflows driven by large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to Building Information Modelling (BIM), enabling natural-language retrieval, modification and generation of IFC models. Recent work has begun adopting the emerging Model Context Protocol (MCP) as a uniform tool-calling interface for LLMs, simplifying the agent side of BIM interaction. While MCP standardises how LLMs invoke tools, current BIM-side implementations are still authoring tool-specific and ad hoc, limiting reuse, evaluation, and workflow portability across environments. This paper addresses this gap by introducing a modular reference architecture for MCP servers that enables API-agnostic, isolated and reproducible agentic BIM interactions. From a systematic analysis of recurring capabilities in recent literature, we derive a core set of requirements. These inform a microservice architecture
[DIGEST] Title: A Modular Reference Architecture for MCP-Servers Enabling Agentic BIM Interaction Abstract: Agentic workflows driven by large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to Building Information Modelling (BIM), enabling natural-language retrieval, modification and generation of IFC models. Within the Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AEC) industry, this trend coincides with the continued shift towards model-centric workflows built on Building Information Modelling (BIM). Most servers follow a monolithic design in which tool invocation, IFC processing , and To address the Methods: The Context Protocol and its application in agentic BIM Research questions and design Recent work on agentic BIM workflows demonstrates substantial progress in manipulation, information retrieval, and validation. This capability gap directly motivates the following research questions: RQ 1: Which core capabilities required for agentic BIM interaction can be identified from existing LLMbased workflows, and how can these be consolidated into a reusable reference architecture? Problem Id en ti fi ca ti on: Common core capabilities and tools As shown in Section 2, among a set of relevant studies on agentic BIM interaction a common core set of tools and generalizable capabilities can be identified. This prevents such capabilities from being replaced, extended, or shared across backends and contributes to a fragmented ecosystem limiting extensibility, comparability, and systematic advancement of agentic BIM research 2. In this work, DSR is implemented by identifying recurring capability needs in recent agentic BIM workflows, designing a modular MCP-based reference architecture to address them, instantiating this architecture through a prototype built around an isolated Ifc Open Shell execution backend, and evaluating it through scenariobased tests focusing on modification and generation tasks. Therefore, a simplified variant of Du et al. 2025’s Text 2 BIM agent is re-implemented, and test cases are assessed to capture complementary aspects of agent behaviour, of existing agentic BIM workflows and emerging MCP-servers for BIM authoring tools and their recurring capability requirements, the proposed architecture follows a set of design principles that guided artefact development. These principles emphasize: (i) Modular microservice decomposition of core capabilities to enable extensibility and substitution of components isolating the generalized MCP-server from the specific tooling; (ii) API-agnosticism to support heterogeneous BIM execution backends; (iii) Isolation of the BIM execution layer for safety and backend flexibility; (iv) Reusable, generic MCP tools at both low and high levels to minimize repeated across heterogeneous BIM backends. The This work introduced a modular reference architecture for MCP servers enabling agentic BIM interaction.
target_text: This paper proposes a modular reference architecture for MCP servers used in agentic BIM workflows. The motivation is that large language models are starting to interact with building information models through the Model Context Protocol, but many current BIM-side implementations are still ad hoc and tied to specific authoring tools. The authors design a microservice-style architecture with an explicit adapter layer so the MCP interface is separated from particular BIM APIs. They also build a prototype around IfcOpenShell and evaluate it on common model-generation and modification tasks. The main takeaway is not a new BIM agent itself, but a reusable systems design that could make LLM-driven BIM interaction more portable, reproducible, and easier to compare across tools.

target_text: This paper tackles the problem of finding every local minimum of a smooth function on a bounded region when the function can only be evaluated approximately. The authors design a probabilistic algorithm that combines polynomial approximation with algebraic system solving to return points whose small neighborhoods contain all local minimizers and keep them separated from one another. In other words, the method does not just find one good answer; it aims to find all of the locally best solutions. The paper also analyzes the algorithm's complexity under stated regularity assumptions and reports experiments showing that their Julia implementation can solve examples that earlier tools could not handle.

[TITLE] Next-Scale Prediction: A Self-Supervised Approach for Real-World Image Denoising
[ABSTRACT] Self-supervised real-world image denoising remains a fundamental challenge, arising from the antagonistic trade-off between decorrelating spatially structured noise and preserving high-frequency details. Existing blind-spot network (BSN) methods rely on pixel-shuffle downsampling (PD) to decorrelate noise, but aggressive downsampling fragments fine structures, while milder downsampling fails to remove correlated noise. To address this, we introduce Next-Scale Prediction (NSP), a novel self-supervised paradigm that decouples noise decorrelation from detail preservation. NSP constructs cross-scale training pairs, where BSN takes low-resolution, fully decorrelated sub-images as input to predict high-resolution targets that retain fine details. As a by-product, NSP naturally supports super-resolution of noisy images without retraining or modification. Extensive experiments demonstrate that NSP achieves state-of-the-art self-supervised denoising performance on real-world benchmarks, significantly alleviating the

The central problem is that existing blind-spot methods must trade off between decorrelating noise and preserving fine visual detail. The authors try to break that trade-off by training the model across image scales: a low-resolution, more decorrelated input is used to predict a higher-resolution target that still contains important structure. The paper reports state-of-the-art results on real-world denoising benchmarks and notes that the same setup can also support super-resolution of noisy images without retraining. In simple terms, the method tries to clean images better while keeping edges and textures sharper.

[ABSTRACT] A black hole is a harmful node in a graph that destroys any agent entering it, making its identification a critical task. In the \emph{Black Hole Search with Verification (BHSV)} problem, a team of agents operates on a graph $G$ with the objective that at least one agent survives and correctly identifies an edge incident to the black hole; if no black hole exists, then all agents must terminate. Prior work has studied BHS in arbitrary dynamic graphs under the restrictive \emph{face-to-face} communication model, where agents can exchange information only when co-located. target_text: This paper studies a search problem on dynamic graphs where one node may act like a 'black hole' that destroys any agent entering it. The goal is to let a team of agents identify an edge touching that dangerous node while still ensuring at least one agent survives, or confirm that no black hole exists. Earlier work mainly assumed very limited communication between agents. Here, the authors strengthen the agents with one-hop visibility, global communication, or both, and show that these extra abilities lead to more efficient solutions. The main contribution is a set of improved algorithms and complexity results showing how better sensing or communication can sharply reduce the resources needed for reliable search in changing networks.

[TITLE] ARMOR: High-Performance Semi-Structured Pruning via Adaptive Matrix Factorization
[ABSTRACT] Large language models (LLMs) present significant deployment challenges due to their immense computational and memory requirements. While semi-structured pruning, particularly 2:4 sparsity, offers a path to practical hardware acceleration, existing methods often incur substantial performance degradation. To bridge this gap, we introduce ARMOR: (Adaptive Representation with Matrix-factORization), a novel one-shot post-training pruning algorithm. Instead of directly pruning weights, ARMOR factorizes each weight matrix into a 2:4 sparse core wrapped by two low-overhead, block diagonal matrices. These wrappers act as efficient pre and post-transformation error correctors, offering greater flexibility to preserve model quality compared to conventional 2:4 pruning techniques. The sparse core and block diagonal wrappers are chosen through a block coordinate descent algorithm that minimizes a layer-wise proxy loss. We

target_text: This paper proposes ARMOR, a pruning method for large language models that tries to make them faster and smaller without hurting accuracy as much as standard sparse pruning. Instead of simply deleting weights, ARMOR factorizes each weight matrix into a sparse core plus two lightweight correction matrices that help repair the damage caused by pruning. The optimization is done in a one-shot post-training setting, so the method is meant to be practical after a model has already been trained. In experiments on Llama and Qwen models, the authors report better task performance and perplexity than earlier 2:4 semi-structured pruning methods while still keeping the hardware-friendly speed and memory benefits of sparse inference.

[TITLE] Automatically Generating Hard Math Problems from Hypothesis-Driven Error Analysis
[ABSTRACT] Numerous math benchmarks exist to evaluate LLMs' mathematical capabilities. However, most involve extensive manual effort and are difficult to scale. Consequently, they cannot keep pace with LLM development or easily provide new instances to mitigate overfitting. Some researchers have proposed automatic benchmark generation methods, but few focus on identifying the specific math concepts and skills on which LLMs are error-prone, and most can only generate category-specific benchmarks. To address these limitations, we propose a new math benchmark generation pipeline that uses AI-generated hypotheses to identify the specific math concepts and skills that LLMs struggle with, and then generates new benchmark problems targeting these weaknesses. target_text: This paper proposes an automated pipeline for generating harder math benchmark problems for large language models. Instead of only creating more examples from broad categories, the method first uses AI-generated hypotheses to identify the specific concepts or skills where a model seems error-prone, then generates new problems targeted at those weaknesses. The authors report that better hypotheses lead to harder generated questions, with the strongest set dropping Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct's accuracy well below its score on the original MATH benchmark. The broader point is that evaluation benchmarks need to evolve along with models. This system aims to create fresher and more diagnostic tests with much less manual effort than traditional benchmark construction.

[TITLE] BadgeX: IoT-Enhanced Wearable Analytics Meets LLMs for Collaborative Learning
[ABSTRACT] We present BadgeX, a novel system integrating lightweight wearable IoT devices (smart badges/smartphones) with Large Language Models (LLMs) to enable real-time collaborative learning analytics. The system captures multimodal sensor data (e.g., audio, image, motion, depth) from learners, processes it into structured features, and employs an LLM-driven framework to interpret these features, generating high-level insights grounded in learning theory. A pilot study demonstrated the system's capability to capture rich collaboration traces and for an LLM to produce plausible, theoretically coherent narrative analyses from sensor-derived features. BadgeX aims to lower deployment barriers, making complex collaborative dynamics visible and offering a pathway for real-time support in educational settings.

target_text: This paper presents BadgeX, a wearable sensing system that combines smart badges or smartphones with large language models to analyze collaboration in real time. The system collects multimodal signals such as audio, images, motion, and depth, converts them into structured features, and then uses an LLM to turn those signals into higher-level narrative feedback grounded in learning theory. A pilot study suggests the platform can capture meaningful traces of group interaction and generate plausible descriptions of how people are collaborating. The larger goal is to make complex teamwork dynamics more visible in educational settings without requiring heavy manual observation, which could eventually support instructors or learners with faster feedback.

[ABSTRACT] The $d$-tilted information has been found to be a useful quantity in finite-blocklength rate-distortion theory for memoryless sources. We study the source-side $d$-tilted sum induced by the single-letter Blahut--Arimoto operating point for a stationary binary Markov source under Hamming distortion; this is a source-side quantity distinct from the $n$-letter operational $d$-tilted information. We show that the centered block sum $J_n(D) - nμ_D$ is exactly an affine image of the occupation count $N_n = \sum_{t=1}^n \mathbf{1}\{X_t = 1\}$ of the Markov chain. target_text: This paper analyzes a quantity from rate-distortion theory for binary Markov sources under Hamming distortion. Rather than focusing on a large operational object, the authors study the single-letter source-side d-tilted sum and show that, after centering, it can be written as a simple affine transform of the Markov chain's occupation count. That link lets them derive several exact results, including closed-form variance, the exact finite-length distribution, and a limiting cumulant generating function via a small transfer matrix. The contribution is mainly mathematical, but the payoff is clarity: the paper shows that the fluctuations of this information-theoretic quantity have a much simpler structure than they may first appear to have.

[TITLE] Comprehensive Analysis of Cellular Uplink Performance in a Dense Stadium Deployment
[ABSTRACT] Uplink performance remains a critical limitation in modern 5G networks, where UEs have to balance limited transmission power against propagation challenges. We conducted extensive measurements in the University of Notre Dame's football stadium, which has a seating capacity of 80,000 spectators, evaluating network behavior under both unloaded (pregame) and severely congested (game day) conditions, with a focus on uplink performance. Analyzing PHY-layer metrics captured via the Rohde & Schwarz QualiPoc, we show that high-frequency TDD bands in the uplink are severely bottlenecked in both the spectral and temporal domains. Despite transmitting near maximum 3GPP power limits, propagation loss inherent to high-frequency bands restricts UEs to low MCS indices and low PRB allocations, even in unloaded networks. target_text: This paper measures 5G uplink performance in a large football stadium and shows that uploading data can remain a major bottleneck even in modern dense deployments. Using field measurements collected before and during a game, the authors find that high-frequency TDD bands struggle in the uplink because phones face strong propagation loss and receive relatively few uplink time slots. As a result, these bands may perform well for downloads but still provide weak upload capacity. The study concludes that lower-frequency FDD bands end up carrying much of the uplink burden. In plain terms, the paper shows why crowded venues can still have poor upload performance even when headline 5G downlink speeds look impressive.

[TITLE] Outlier-Robust Nonlinear Moving Horizon Estimation using Adaptive Loss Functions
[ABSTRACT] In this work, we propose an adaptive robust loss function framework for MHE, integrating an adaptive robust loss function to reduce the impact of outliers with a regularization term that avoids naive solutions. The proposed approach prioritizes the fitting of uncontaminated data and downweights the contaminated ones. A tuning parameter is incorporated into the framework to control the shape of the loss function for adjusting the estimator's robustness to outliers. The simulation results demonstrate that adaptation occurs in just a few iterations, whereas the traditional behaviour $\mathrm{L_2}$ predominates when the measurements are free of outliers.

target_text: This paper proposes a nonlinear moving horizon estimation method that is more robust to outliers. The core idea is to replace a fixed quadratic loss with an adaptive robust loss function, while adding regularization so the estimator does not fall into trivial solutions. A tuning parameter lets the method adjust how aggressively it downweights suspicious measurements. In simulations, the approach quickly adapts when outliers appear, but behaves more like standard L2 estimation when the data are clean. The main contribution is a practical estimation framework that can switch between accuracy and robustness more flexibly, which is useful in control and monitoring settings where occasional bad measurements can otherwise distort the state estimate.

target_text: This paper studies machine-learning attacks on the Learning With Errors problem, a core hardness assumption in lattice-based cryptography. Prior ML attacks worked best on relatively sparse secrets. The authors show that using larger datasets and repeated examples can push those attacks toward denser secrets, and they describe an empirical power-law relationship between recovery success, dataset size, and repetition. They also introduce a stepwise regression method meant to recover additional parts of the secret. The work does not break modern cryptography outright, but it does probe where some LWE variants may become more vulnerable, which is useful for stress-testing post-quantum systems and understanding the limits of ML-based cryptanalysis.

target_text: This paper introduces FAVE, a recommendation method designed to generate the next item in a user sequence more efficiently than diffusion-style approaches. The authors argue that many flow-based recommenders still waste computation because they start from uninformative noise and spend effort modeling transitions that are partly deterministic. FAVE instead learns a direct one-step trajectory from a more informative starting point, using a two-stage training process that first builds a stable semantic space and then adds a semantic anchor prior plus a learned average velocity. The goal is to keep strong recommendation quality while reducing generation cost. In simple terms, the paper tries to make sequence recommendation both faster and more purposeful by starting closer to where the answer should be.

[TITLE] Subset Balancing and Generalized Subset Sum via Lattices
[ABSTRACT] We study the \emph{Subset Balancing} problem: given $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{Z}^n$ and a coefficient set $C \subseteq \mathbb{Z}$, find a nonzero vector $\mathbf{c} \in C^n$ such that $\mathbf{c}\cdot\mathbf{x} = 0$. The standard meet-in-the-middle algorithm runs in time $\tilde{O}(|C|^{n/2})=\tilde{O}(2^{n\log |C|/2})$, and recent improvements (SODA~2022, Chen, Jin, Randolph, and Servedio; STOC~2026, Randolph and Węgrzycki) beyond this barrier apply mainly when $d$ is constant. We give a reduction from Subset Balancing with $C = \{-d, \dots, d\}$ to a single instance of $\mathrm{SVP}_{\infty}$ in dimension $n+1$, which yields a deterministic algorithm with running time $\tilde{O}((6\sqrt{2πe})^n) \approx \tilde{O}(2^{4.632n})$, and a randomized algorithm with running time $\tilde{O}(2^{2.443n})$ (here $\tilde{O}$ suppresses $\operatorname{poly}(n)$ factors). We also show that for sufficiently large $d$, Subset Balancing is solvable in polynomial

target_text: This paper studies the Subset Balancing problem and gives new algorithms based on lattice reductions. The key result is a reduction from subset balancing with coefficients in a bounded integer range to a single instance of an infinity-norm shortest vector problem. Using the best known lattice algorithms for that target problem, the authors obtain running times that depend on the dimension but no longer pay the usual log d cost in the exponent. For sufficiently large coefficient ranges, they also show the problem becomes polynomial-time solvable. The paper is mainly a complexity-theory advance, but the practical message is that a careful geometric reformulation can lead to substantially better algorithms than the classic meet-in-the-middle approach for many parameter settings.
